round_notes: One round-1 finding was materially wrong in the user's favor: the characterization of atuin-ai as a remote LLM that can instruct the CLI to run shell commands, read files, or write files, defended primarily by a permission prompt. Source reading in round 2 (see F001) shows that for `execute_shell_command`, `read_file`, and `create_file`, a client-hardcoded capability allowlist gates the tool at the transport layer before the permission prompt ever runs, and those capabilities are not advertised in any default or user-facing configuration. The permission prompt is the second line of defense, not the first. F001 supersedes the round-1 AI-subsystem assessment. F002 (sync censorship) and F003 (daemon peer-cred) are new findings surfaced by the round-2 source trace and do not correspond to prior round-1 findings.

findings.verdict: The atuin-ai subsystem gates remote-instructed shell/read/write tool calls at the transport layer via a client-hardcoded capability allowlist that advertises only `client_v1_atuin_history` in any default or user-configurable configuration, placing capability enforcement ahead of the user-facing permission prompt.
findings.rationale:
Three layers of defense exist, checked top-down on each incoming SSE tool-call frame:

**Layer 1 — tool name parsing** (`tools/mod.rs:320-333`). `ClientToolCall::try_from((name, input))` accepts exactly the names `read_file`, `create_file`, `execute_shell_command`, `atuin_history`. Anything else returns an `Err` result. Hard, enum-bounded set compiled into the binary.

**Layer 2 — capability gating** (`stream.rs:289-311`). Each parsed tool call must have its `descriptor().capability` present in the session's advertised capability list or the frame is rejected with a canned error `tool_result`. Rejection happens before the tool is added to the tool tracker and before any permission-check event is emitted. The server sees an error; the user sees nothing.

**Layer 3 — capability list construction** (`stream.rs:62-87`). The only automatic capability advertised is `client_v1_atuin_history` when `ai.capabilities.enable_history_search.unwrap_or(true)`. The `AiCapabilities` struct has exactly one field (`enable_history_search`); there is no config-surface flag for shell/read/write. A second code path reads an undocumented environment variable `ATUIN_AI__ADDITIONAL_CAPS` and splits comma-separated values into additional caps. That is the only reachable mechanism for widening the surface short of a future release.

**Net effect on a stock install.** Of the four compiled-in client tool types, only `atuin_history` is reachable. `execute_shell_command`, `read_file`, and `create_file` are rejected at the transport layer. The permission prompt is the second line of defense for `atuin_history`, not the first line for any of the others.

**Why this is a positive finding.** Round 1 concluded the AI subsystem was a remote-instructed local agent with file/shell tools defended primarily by a permission prompt. That conclusion is wrong for three of the four tools. The realistic threat in a default configuration is a hostile Hub issuing `atuin_history` queries under the user's permission prompts — still an exposure if `enable_history_search` stays on (default true), but a materially narrower one.

**Caveats.** The env-var escape hatch (`ATUIN_AI__ADDITIONAL_CAPS`) means anything in the user's shell environment that sets it silently widens the surface. Worth a `ps e` / `env` check if suspicious.

citations.quoted: `ChatRequest::new`: appends `client_v1_atuin_history` only if enabled; also reads `ATUIN_AI__ADDITIONAL_CAPS` env var as the sole escape hatch
capability gating: if `required_cap` not in `capabilities`, emit error `tool_result` and return before tracker insert or permission-check dispatch
`ClientToolCall::try_from` match over compiled-in wire names — enum-bounded
`AiCapabilities { enable_history_search: Option<bool> }` — single-field struct, no config surface for shell/read/write

findings.verdict: A compromised sync server can silently censor individual records because the client's download loop counts received records rather than verifying contiguous idx ranges, stores arrivals via insert-or-ignore without monotonicity assertions, and thereafter reports a fully-synced state based on local max(idx).
findings.rationale:
PASETO v4 Local implicit assertions bind `id`, `idx`, `tag`, `host`, `version` into each record's AEAD (`encryption.rs:170-196`). The server cannot tamper with the content of a specific idx — forged idx would fail decryption authentication. It can, however, drop records.

The download loop (`record/sync.rs:218-273`) requests pages starting at `local + progress`, stores whatever it receives via `push_batch`, and increments `progress += page.len()` — a count of records received, not a validation of idx-range coverage. When a page is empty, the loop breaks. No assertion that `progress == expected` at termination.

The local SQLite push (`sqlite_store.rs:73-94`) uses `insert or ignore into store(...)` with no constraint that idx be contiguous per (host, tag). Gaps persist.

On the next sync, `status()` (`sqlite_store.rs:264+`) returns `max(idx)` per (host, tag). If local received idx [0,1,2,7,8,9] because a server dropped 3-6, local max is 9, and the client believes it is fully synced. The gap is never rediscovered, even if the server later starts serving the missing records.

**Threat model.** Requires adversary control of the sync server (atuinsh for the default address, the user for self-hosters). Not reachable by a passive network attacker. The README claim of encrypted sync is accurate for confidentiality but does not cover integrity-in-the-availability-sense.

**Fix shape.** A per-record hash chain or Merkle structure that the client verifies on download would close this. Neither exists today.

citations.quoted: `sync_download`: `progress += page.len() as u64` — counts records, not idx coverage; loop terminates on empty page without validating range
`insert or ignore into store(...)` — no contiguity or monotonicity enforcement
`Assertions<'a>` binds id/idx/tag/host/version via implicit assertion — prevents tampering, not censorship

findings.verdict: The atuin-daemon accepts connections on its Unix socket (or Windows loopback TCP port) without peer-UID verification, explicit socket permission hardening, or any tonic/tower authentication layer over its gRPC services.
findings.rationale:
`server.rs:67-70` binds the Unix socket at the configured path and returns it to the tonic server builder. No `std::fs::set_permissions(..., PermissionsExt::from_mode(0o600))` call follows the bind; socket permissions inherit from umask. No `SO_PEERCRED` / `getpeereid` read appears in any Interceptor or middleware.

On Windows (`server.rs:128-134`), the daemon binds `127.0.0.1:${tcp_port}` (default 8889) with no authentication. Loopback-only narrows the surface to same-host attackers.

The three gRPC services (`HistoryServer`, `SearchServer`, `ControlServer`) are attached with no auth middleware. `ControlServer` includes a shutdown RPC; anything that can connect can also stop the daemon.

**Context dependence.** On a single-user machine with umask 022 and a 0700 parent data directory (typical default), the socket is reachable only by the owning user because the *directory* is restrictive, even though the socket file itself is not. On a shared host with a looser data-dir umask, or on a multi-user Windows box, any local process can speak the full gRPC surface.

**Fix shape.** `chmod 0o600` after bind for defense-in-depth on the socket itself, plus a tonic Interceptor that reads SO_PEERCRED and rejects cross-UID callers. Both cheap.

citations.quoted: Windows TCP fallback binds `127.0.0.1:${tcp_port}` with no auth middleware

positive_absences.pattern_checked: presence of `unsafe` blocks in atuin-client and atuin-ai crates
positive_absences.description: Rust memory-safety escape hatches in security-critical paths — encryption, sync, AI tool dispatch. Ripgrep for the `unsafe` keyword across both crates returned zero matches. The project relies on safe Rust throughout its hot paths, so memory-safety guarantees hold across the code paths that handle ciphertext, SSE parsing, and tool parameter deserialization.

citations.quoted: crate root — ripgrep scan scope anchor; no `unsafe` found in the tree
crate root — ripgrep scan scope anchor; no `unsafe` found in the tree

positive_absences.pattern_checked: server-side plain-text password storage or weak password hashing
positive_absences.description: Registration handler appears to hash passwords via bcrypt before storage. Spot-checked the code path; no plain-text retention or weak-hash variant observed. Not a full crypto review — timing-safe comparison on login, argon2 migration path, and password-reset flow were not audited.

citations.quoted: registration handler file — spot-check scope; deeper review not performed

methodology_trace.notes: Twelve patterns spanning the collector-hint spectrum, drawn from the round-2 §B catalog. Some are one-hop greps (MP-IPC-02, MP-FILE-01); some require multi-hop tracing through transport → parsing → dispatch (MP-CAP-01); some narrow a universe but need human or LLM triage (MP-NET-02, MP-ENV-01). The `hit_on_target` field marks which patterns actually produced a finding in this engagement, so the catalog doubles as validation data for future collector implementations.

patterns.description: Hardcoded URLs in production code paths that should route through the sync_address / server_address configuration.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Hits in tests, doc comments, and help-text strings produce noise. Scope to function bodies by filtering lines preceded by `///` or inside `#[cfg(test)]` blocks.

patterns.description: Network fetches triggered by startup, login, or config-load rather than by explicit user action — potential background-call surface.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Every fetch matches; collector narrows the universe but human or LLM triage needed to distinguish 'happens during update-check tick' from 'happens because user ran atuin login'.

patterns.description: Telemetry or update-check endpoints that bypass the user-configured server, leaking requests to a vendor host even in self-hosted setups.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Not grep-automatable: requires tracing from user-facing action to URL-construction and noticing the URL is NOT derived from settings. Surfaces as a finding only on source review.

patterns.description: TcpListener / UnixListener bind followed by serve without an authentication middleware or peer-credential check on accepted connections.
patterns.false_positive_notes: The bind itself is trivially findable; whether an Interceptor is attached downstream requires following the Server::builder() chain.

patterns.description: UnixListener::bind that is not followed by an explicit std::fs::set_permissions / PermissionsExt::from_mode call in the same function.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Permissions may be set on the parent directory rather than the socket; collector should flag the shape, not claim exploit.

patterns.description: File creation for a path named `key`, `token`, `session`, or `credentials` without an explicit 0o600 permission set nearby.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Misses OpenOptions::new().write(true).create(true).open(...) idioms and tokio::fs::File::create; extend the pattern accordingly.

patterns.description: env::temp_dir() joined with an attacker-influenceable string, creating a predictable /tmp path on Linux shared hosts.
patterns.false_positive_notes: On macOS temp_dir() is user-private; severity varies by platform. Collector should note platform-conditional severity.

patterns.description: Option<bool> feature flag consumed via .unwrap_or(true) — defaults-to-enabled behavior that a config audit might miss.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Hits on every unwrap_or(true); narrows only slightly. Cross-reference against struct field names containing `enabled` or capability-verb nouns to prioritize.

patterns.description: std::env::var reads that conditionally widen security-relevant behavior — appending to a capability list, disabling TLS verification, bypassing auth — without being documented in the config surface.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Every env::var read matches; the reasoning step is whether the branch it enables weakens any security invariant. Most env reads are benign (HOME, XDG_CONFIG_HOME, ATUIN_SESSION).

patterns.description: Remote-instructed tool dispatch (SSE, MCP, etc.) that does not gate incoming tool names against a client-hardcoded enum before permission resolution. Miss case = the client blindly dispatches on server-chosen strings.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Not detectable by grep alone; requires tracing the SSE/MCP frame handler to the tool-call dispatch and verifying a bounded match{} on tool names. LLM-grade analysis task.

patterns.description: deny.toml / audit.toml present at repo root but not invoked by any CI workflow step — declared-but-unenforced policy.
patterns.false_positive_notes: External CI (Branch Protection required-checks, GitHub Apps) can enforce without leaving a trace in-repo; collector should flag as probable-unenforced, not definitive.

patterns.description: cargo publish absent from any CI workflow when the project is published to crates.io — implies local-maintainer publish, exposing the crates.io API token on at least one laptop.
patterns.false_positive_notes: Some projects publish via cargo-release with OIDC; collector should check for that variant too. Absence of both patterns is the real signal.
